mainEntity.name: Can a phone really take a professional-looking headshot?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Modern phone cameras with portrait mode produce headshots that hold up perfectly well on LinkedIn, company websites and most professional use cases. The camera is not the limiting factor. Light, background and expression are. Get those right with a phone and you will beat most badly-lit DSLR shots you see online.

mainEntity.name: What is the best time of day for a home headshot?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the UK, between 10am and 2pm on a cloudy or lightly overcast day is ideal. Direct sunlight streaming through a window creates hard shadows. Overcast British weather acts as a natural softbox and gives you soft, even light without you needing to do anything.

mainEntity.name: Which direction should my window face?

acceptedAnswer.text: North-facing windows are the gold standard because they never receive direct sunlight. East, west and south-facing windows all work but need managing — either wait for the sun to move past, or diffuse the light with a white bedsheet or sheer curtain. Avoid shooting directly into a window that the sun is hitting.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a proper reflector or can I use something from the house?

acceptedAnswer.text: A white towel, a large white pillowcase or a sheet of A2 white paper will do the same job as a £20 reflector for your first few shoots. If you find yourself shooting headshots regularly, a collapsible 5-in-1 reflector is a sensible upgrade because it gives you five different surfaces in one portable package.

mainEntity.name: How do I stop mixed colour temperatures ruining the photo?

acceptedAnswer.text: Turn every indoor light off. Overhead bulbs, lamps, even the TV in the corner. Mixing daylight with tungsten bulbs produces a photo where one cheek looks yellow and the other looks blue, and it is almost impossible to fix cleanly in editing. Clean, single-source daylight is always the easier path.

mainEntity.name: Should I use the selfie camera on my phone?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Selfie cameras use wide-angle lenses that distort facial features — your nose looks larger, your face looks rounder. Always use the rear camera, set it on a tripod or stack of books at eye level, and trigger it with the timer or a remote. The improvement is immediate and obvious.

mainEntity.name: Are AI headshot generators a viable alternative?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not for a professional profile where trust matters. AI-generated headshots have a distinctive flat, over-smooth quality that recruiters and clients increasingly recognise. Some also subtly alter your features. For a LinkedIn profile or company website, a real window-light photograph of your actual face is worth more than any AI composite.

mainEntity.name: When should I book a professional photographer instead?

acceptedAnswer.text: Book a pro if you need consistent headshots for an entire team, if you are in a senior executive role where appearance is part of the job, if you have a tight deadline, or if you need multiple looks and full retouching in one sitting. Expect to pay £150 to £400 for a solo session in the UK.
